title: 1RM Back Squat Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Free 1RM back squat calculator using Epley formula. Quickly estimate your max lift for strength training.
intro: The Back Squat 1-Rep Max (1RM) Calculator estimates the maximum weight you can lift for a single repetition based on a submaximal set — a weight you can move for 2–10 reps with good form. The most widely used formula is the **Epley equation**: `1RM = weight × (1 + reps / 30)`. For example, squatting 80 kg for 5 reps predicts a 1RM of ≈ 93 kg. Knowing your 1RM lets you program training zones, set competition openers, and track long-term strength progress without the injury risk of an all-out single every week.
keyTakeaway: **1RM = Weight (kg) × (1 + Reps / 30)** — e.g., 100 kg × 8 reps → 1RM ≈ 127 kg; use 3–6 rep sets for the most accurate predictions (±2–5%).
useCases: Programming squat percentages for a 12-week powerlifting peaking block (e.g., Week 1 at 70% 1RM = 6×4 sets, Week 10 at 90% 1RM = 3×2 sets)
Setting opening, second, and third attempts at a USAPL or IPF sanctioned powerlifting meet (openers typically 90–92% predicted 1RM)
Calculating load for hypertrophy work in the 65–75% 1RM range (8–12 reps) after a tested 5-rep max in the back squat
Tracking strength gains over a training cycle by re-testing a 5RM or 3RM every 4–6 weeks and comparing estimated 1RMs without grinding a true max lift
Prescribing rehabilitation loading targets after a lower-body injury, where a direct 1RM test is contraindicated but relative intensity must be controlled

fields.label: Weight (kg)
fields.help: Load on the bar in kg.

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator uses the **Epley (1985)** formula, the most widely validated single-equation estimator for the squat:

```
1RM = weight × (1 + reps / 30)
```

Where:
- **weight** = load lifted in kg (or lb — keep units consistent)
- **reps** = number of repetitions completed (best accuracy: 2–10 reps)

**Step-by-step example — 80 kg × 5 reps:**
```
1RM = 80 × (1 + 5/30)
    = 80 × (1 + 0.1667)
    = 80 × 1.1667
    = 93.3 kg  →  rounded to 93 kg
```

Other validated formulas you may encounter:
| Formula | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Epley (1985) | `w × (1 + r/30)` | Default; best for r ≤ 10 |
| Brzycki (1993) | `w × 36 / (37 − r)` | Slightly more conservative |
| Lombardi (1989) | `w × r^0.10` | Tends to overestimate at high reps |
| O'Conner (1989) | `w × (1 + 0.025 × r)` | Lower bound estimate |

---

## Reference Table

Estimated 1RM from common working sets (Epley formula):

| Weight Lifted | Reps | Estimated 1RM | % of True 1RM Accuracy* |
|---|---|---|---|
| 60 kg | 3 | 66 kg | ~98% |
| 60 kg | 5 | 70 kg | ~96% |
| 80 kg | 3 | 88 kg | ~98% |
| 80 kg | 5 | 93 kg | ~96% |
| 80 kg | 8 | 101 kg | ~93% |
| 100 kg | 3 | 110 kg | ~98% |
| 100 kg | 5 | 117 kg | ~96% |
| 100 kg | 10 | 133 kg | ~90% |
| 120 kg | 5 | 140 kg | ~96% |
| 140 kg | 3 | 154 kg | ~98% |

*Accuracy vs. directly tested 1RM; based on meta-analytic data from Mayhew et al. (1992) and Reynolds et al. (2006).

---

## Typical Use Cases with Numbers

**Case 1 — Intermediate lifter, hypertrophy block:**
An athlete squats 95 kg × 8 reps to technical failure.
`1RM = 95 × (1 + 8/30) = 95 × 1.267 = 120 kg`
Training zones derived: 65% = 78 kg, 75% = 90 kg, 85% = 102 kg.

**Case 2 — Meet preparation:**
A powerlifter hits 160 kg × 3 in training five weeks out.
`1RM = 160 × (1 + 3/30) = 160 × 1.10 = 176 kg`
Suggested meet openers: ~90% = 158 kg; second attempt: 168 kg; third: 176 kg+.

**Case 3 — Beginner tracking progress over 8 weeks:**
Week 1: 50 kg × 5 → `1RM = 50 × 1.167 = 58 kg`
Week 8: 65 kg × 5 → `1RM = 65 × 1.167 = 76 kg`
Estimated 1RM gain: +18 kg (+31%) without ever testing a true single.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Using too many reps (> 10):** The Epley formula degrades sharply above 10 reps. A set of 15 reps at 60 kg gives `1RM = 60 × 1.5 = 90 kg`, but the true 1RM is likely closer to 78–82 kg — an overestimate of 10–15%. Always use sets of 3–8 for reliable predictions.

2. **Not reaching true effort on the test set:** If you stop at 5 reps when you had 4 more in the tank, your estimated 1RM will be artificially low. The formula assumes the rep count is at or near technical failure (≤1–2 reps in reserve).

3. **Mixing up units mid-calculation:** Entering weight in pounds but interpreting the output as kilograms inflates the estimate by 2.2×. The formula is unit-agnostic — use the same unit in and out.

4. **Applying the estimate to a cold, untrained state:** A predicted 1RM from a well-warmed-up working set will not match an unwarmed max. Always ensure test conditions (warm-up, rest) reflect the context in which you'll use the 1RM.

5. **Ignoring individual variability:** Research shows prediction errors of ±5–10% between individuals, driven by fiber-type distribution and neuromuscular efficiency. Treat the output as a starting estimate, not a guarantee.

---

## Related Calculators

- [Wilks Score Calculator](/wilks-score-calculator) — converts raw squat total to bodyweight-adjusted score for powerlifting comparisons
- [Body Mass Index (BMI) Calculator](/calculadora-imc) — useful context for strength-to-bodyweight ratio benchmarks
- [Calories Burned Calculator](/calorias-ejercicio) — estimate energy expenditure for a squat training session

faq.q: How accurate is the Epley formula for the back squat?
faq.a: Studies (Mayhew et al., 1992; Reynolds et al., 2006) show mean prediction errors of 2–5% when using 3–8 rep sets. Accuracy drops to ±10–15% for sets above 10 reps. For most training purposes, an error of ±3–5 kg on a 100 kg squat is operationally acceptable.

faq.q: What rep range gives the most accurate 1RM prediction?
faq.a: The consensus in the strength science literature is **3–6 repetitions**. At this range, the Epley, Brzycki, and O'Conner formulas all converge within 2–3% of each other and of the directly measured 1RM. Above 8 reps, divergence between formulas exceeds 5%, reducing reliability.

faq.q: Should I test my actual 1RM or just use this calculator?
faq.a: For most recreational athletes, the estimated 1RM is safer and sufficient. A true 1RM test carries elevated injury risk, requires a full warm-up protocol (typically 45–60 min), and needs a spotter or power rack. Reserve direct 1RM testing for competition prep or formal strength assessments every 8–12 weeks.

faq.q: Can I use this formula for other barbell lifts like the deadlift or bench press?
faq.a: Yes — the Epley formula is lift-agnostic and is routinely applied to the bench press, deadlift, overhead press, and front squat. However, accuracy can vary by lift: it tends to be slightly less precise for the deadlift (where rep maxes >5 are common) and quite reliable for the bench press at 3–6 reps.

faq.q: What percentage of 1RM should I use for different training goals?
faq.a: NSCA guidelines recommend: **Strength** = 85–100% 1RM (1–6 reps); **Power/Speed-Strength** = 75–85% (3–5 reps); **Hypertrophy** = 67–85% (6–12 reps); **Muscular Endurance** = <67% (15+ reps). These zones apply directly to the estimated 1RM from this calculator.

faq.q: Does bodyweight affect how accurate the prediction is?
faq.a: Heavier athletes (>100 kg) and very experienced lifters may see the formula slightly overestimate their 1RM because they tend to have a lower rep-max-to-1RM ratio, likely due to a higher proportion of fast-twitch fibers. Lightweight or novice lifters generally see predictions within 2–3% of their actual max.

faq.q: How often should I re-test my estimated 1RM during a training cycle?
faq.a: Most periodization models (e.g., Wendler 5/3/1, Texas Method) re-test or reset 1RM estimates every **4–8 weeks**. A standard approach: after completing a 4-week block, perform a 3RM or 5RM test set and recalculate. For a 16-week powerlifting prep, formal 1RM testing is typically done only at the competition itself.

faq.q: Is there a difference between high-bar and low-bar squat 1RMs?
faq.a: Yes. The low-bar back squat (used in powerlifting) allows most trained lifters to move **5–12% more weight** than the high-bar squat due to greater hip drive and a shorter moment arm at the knee. If you train high-bar but compete low-bar, apply a 5–10% upward adjustment to your estimated competition 1RM — and vice versa.

sources.name: NSCA – Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning (Free NSCA Resources)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
